steps.text: Purchase precleaned amber glass jars and petri dishes, or clean according to the glassware cleaning procedures listed in Section 8.1.1.1 of this method.
Use a weighing device capable of measurements to an accuracy of 0.5 g.
Use heavy duty aluminum foil cleaned by rinsing three times with hexane or toluene and stored in a pre-cleaned glass petri dish or glass jar.
Do not use aluminum foil to wrap or contact filter samples due to the possibility of reaction between the sample and the aluminum.
Use an air-tight container to store silica gel.
Recover samples in amber glass bottles, 500- or 1000-milliliters (mL) with leak-free PTFE-lined caps.
Either purchase precleaned bottles or clean containers according to glassware cleaning procedures listed in Section 8.1.1.1 of this method.
Use 125- and 250-mL amber glass bottles with PTFE-lined caps.
Use glass test tubes or small (e.g., 5 to 10 mL) amber vials.
Use 200-mL capacity thimble holder capable of holding 43 × 123-millimeter (mm) extraction thimbles, with receiving flask (typically round-bottom).
Use Dean-Stark or Barret with fluoropolymer stopcock trap to fit between the Soxhlet extractor body and the condenser as shown in Figure 23–3 of this method.
Use quartz, glass, or glass fiber thimble, typically 43 × 123 mm to fit Soxhlet apparatus.
The use of cellulose thimbles for sample extraction in this method is prohibited.
Use a hemispherical shaped heating mantle to fit round-bottom flask.
Evaluate alternative carbon-based sorbents for this purpose prior to their use.
Prepare spiking standards quantitatively at a convenient concentration (e.g., 10 nanograms (ng)/mL) or use commercial standards if available.
Prepare stock standard solutions in nonane to enable spiking for Pre-Sampling Adsorbent Standard.
Prepare stock standard solutions in nonane to enable spiking for Pre-extraction Filter Recovery Standard.
Bake glassware up to 400 °C for a minimum of 2 hours to remove contaminants or interferents from particularly dirty samples.
Allow glassware to cool after baking.
Cover glassware openings with clean glass fitting caps or cleaned aluminum foil.
Rinse glassware immediately before use with acetone and toluene.

Prepare heavily soiled glassware by removing surface residuals from the glassware by soaking in hot soapy water, rinsing with hot water, then soaking with a non-chromic acid oxidizing cleaning reagent in a strong acid.
After the acid soak, rinse with hot water and repeat the cleaning procedures.
Load adsorbent modules in a clean area to avoid contamination.
Fill a module with 20 to 40 g of XAD–2.
Spike modules before the sampling event, but do not spike the modules in the field.
Add the pre-sampling adsorbent standard to the top quarter of the adsorbent bed.
Add sufficient spike to ensure the standard recovery is quantitative.
Follow the XAD–2 with cleaned glass wool and tightly cap both ends of the module.
Use spiked modules within 14 days of preparation.

Quantitatively recover impinger water samples for analysis if PAH and/or PCB are the target compounds by rinsing three times with acetone followed by three rinses with toluene. Collect impinger water and rinses in Container No. 3 and mark the level of the liquid on the container.
Note the color of the indicating silica gel to determine if it has been completely spent and report its condition on the field data sheet.
Store all field samples temporarily in cool (≤20 °C, 68 °F) and dark conditions prior to transport to the laboratory.
Ship samples cool (≤20 °C, 68 °F), shielded from ultraviolet light.

Follow the procedures in American Society for Testing and Materials (ASTM) D6911–15 (Guide for Packaging and Shipping Environmental Samples for Laboratory Analysis) for all samples, where appropriate.
Pay special attention to cleanliness during transport, field handling, sampling, recovery, and laboratory analysis, as well as during preparation of the adsorbent cartridges.
Follow the chain of custody procedures in ASTM D4840–99(2018)e1 (Standard Guide for Sample Chain-of-Custody Procedures) for all samples (including field samples and blanks).
Store sampling train rinses and filter samples in the dark at the storage conditions in Table 23–10 from the time the laboratory receives the samples until analysis.
Store adsorbent samples for PCDD/PCDF or PCB analysis prior to extraction in the dark at 6 °C (43 °F) or less for up to one year from the time of collection.
Perform initial calibration following the procedures in Section 10.
Establish a target concentration for the lowest calibration standard based on the intended use of the method.
Perform seven replicate analyses of a calibration sample prepared at proposed lowest calibration concentration.
Calculate the mean and standard deviation for each analyte in these replicates.
Confirm the laboratory’s ability to measure analyte concentrations down to the lowest calibration point.
Pack a column (20 mm ID, 300 mm in length) with approximately 6 to 12 g of basic alumina.
Pre-elute the column with 50 to 100 mL of hexane.
Transfer the concentrated extract from the previous column cleanup to the top of the basic alumina column.
Allow the sample to flow into the column leaving a small quantity of solvent above the top of the bed.
Rinse the extract container with two additional 1-mL rinses of hexane and apply each rinse to the column separately as the previous addition elutes.
Elute the column with 100 mL hexane to remove the interferences.
Elute the PCDD/PCDF from the column with 20 to 40 mL of 50% methylene chloride in hexane.
Concentrate the eluate to approximately 0.5 mL using the nitrogen evaporative concentrator.
Cut both ends from a 10 mL disposable Pasteur pipette to produce a 10 cm column.
Fire-polish both ends and flare both ends if desired.
Insert a glass wool plug at one end and pack the column with 0.55 g of Carbopack®/Celite® to form an adsorbent bed approximately 2 cm long.
Insert a glass wool plug on top of the bed to hold the adsorbent in place.
Pre-elute the column with 5 mL of toluene followed by 2 mL of methylene chloride:methanol:toluene (15:4:1 volume/volume (v/v)).
Add 1 mL of methylene chloride:cyclohexane (1:1 v/v) and 5 mL of hexane.
If the flow rate of eluate exceeds 0.5 mL/minute, discard the column.
Do not let the head of the column go without solvent.
Add the sample extract to the column.
Rinse the sample container twice with 1 mL portions of hexane and apply separately to the column.
Analyze sample (or another aliquot of the sample) using an alternative column that provides elution order to uniquely quantify the target compounds subject to interference on the first GC column.
Document the required resolution when using a confirmation column.
Prepare and analyze a spiked sample (spiked at 1 to 5 times the determined MDL).
Confirm that the target compounds meet the qualitative identification criteria.
Repeat the MDL determination with a higher spike concentration if the MDL confirmation criteria are not met.
Tune the instrument to meet the required resolving power for the desired target compound class.
Assess the resolution at three exact m/z’s representing the low-, mid-, and high-m/z range of the masses used to measure the target compound class.
Use peak matching and the chosen PFK (or FC43) reference peak to verify that the exact mass is within 5 ppm of the required value.
If the recovery criteria are not met, you may reanalyze the sample and/or prepare and analyze the archive sample.
If the recovery criteria are still not met, the sampling run is not valid, and the stack test must be repeated.
If the minimum response is not met, you must reanalyze the sample.
If these criteria are not met, the lowest calibration point has been set too low and must be confirmed at a higher concentration.
Demonstration would be repeated for failed compounds only.
The average % recovery for each target analyte must be within 70 to 130%.

constraints.text: Samples and extracts must be stored in the dark.

steps.text: Remove both the salt solution and any residual extractable chemicals used in the polymerization process before use.
Prepare the resin by a series of water and organic extractions, followed by careful drying.
Perform the extraction using a Soxhlet extractor or other apparatus that generates resin meeting the requirements in Section 13.1 of Method 23.
Use an all-glass thimble containing an extra-coarse frit for extraction of the resin.
Place resin in a suitable container, soak for approximately 5 min with Type II water, remove fine floating resin particles and discard the water.
Fill with Type II water a second time, let stand overnight, remove fine floating resin particles, and discard the water.
